item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I got the idea, but in the end the lack of something concrete let me feeling nothing here. What are the triggers for the dream? How do the clues appear? Not necessarily how it happens, but at the very least at which context?

For me it ended up being too formless to be convinced and start the mood process.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: this should NOT have been the scp I decided to read after waking up at 4 am. the implications of the tapes scare me so much, does the hole actually exist? or is this like a recording of a dream? and knowing that after I read the article, I had been exposed to it. and well, now I don't want to go back to sleep. a reluctant 1+ for spooking the shit out of me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The entry would be a lot better if we got info about what the "components" were in the description section. Seems pretty weird to not describe the component parts of the SCP in the actual description area and it makes the rest of the entry feel very vague.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Meta shit usually doesn't really get me anymore but holy shit.

Edit: I think about the short story "BLIT" by David Langford a lot. The ending of this really reminds me of the ending of that. Get some alcohol, try not to piece the clues together to form a cohesive image—

item.title: Re: Zuul is that you?
item.content:encoded: I just wanted to mention that "Gozer Logic" is quite possibly my favorite summation of how this SCP works.

item.title: Zuul is that you?
item.content:encoded:
This made my head spin the first time I read it, until I read that the hole will generate a monster based off of scary stories/reports/memories/nightmares/events and fuse them together to make something that wants to kill you. Then I realized something: This thing works on gozer logic!
What I mean by that is:

  • It requires a source to create a physical body
  • If someone thinks/remembers something, it will assume that form
  • it doesn't matter what is thought of, as long as it holds importance (Scary thoughts that aren't scary for some/Childhood icon)
  • the physical manifestation of the thought will attempt to get you no matter what.

Basically, the first thing I thought of when some people pointed it out was(And I'm Paraphrasing Peter Venkman here): "Oh I get it! Very cute! so what ever we find scary, if someone found J. Edgar Hoover scary, J. Edgar Hoover will appear and take them.so empty your heads! empty your heads we got one shot at this!

item.title: Re: Author's Post
item.content:encoded: Thanks to SunnyClockwork for making this excellent art of SCP-3117!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1 - great tense storytelling and great visuals that managed to remain incredibly spooky without using any ooga booga scary faces. Love the meta bit at the end revealing that the monster's coming to get YOU - it's a bit if of a creepypasta cliche but still feels perfectly executed.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
All the hub/MTF links are fine, but SCP-3089's bot is actually I/O-SILVER, not I/O-SPIRAL. I'm comfortable with this article's bot (I/O-SPIRAL) being renamed I/O-SILVER to facilitate the crosslink, though; I/O-SPIRAL isn't something I used anywhere else, and I'm an unrepentant fan of the I/O-SILVER pun.

(honestly all of my skips are just wafer-thin justifications for making terrible puns)

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

A few in-line links to propose here. First off, Hippo has used the bot I/O-Spiral in a few of their articles, namely SCP-3089. The mention here can easily in-line link there, and facilitate a good wikiwalk due to that article also having several links. Additionally, the mention of MTF Iota-10 and the GOC could in-line link to their respective hubs. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

A Foundation-operated bot (I/O-SPIRAL) is to monitor online communities for discussions regarding SCP-3117. MTF Phi-11 ("Promise-Keepers") is to investigate these discussions and make a determination for appropriate action on a case-by-case basis.

To this:

A Foundation-operated bot (I/O-SPIRAL) is to monitor online communities for discussions regarding SCP-3117. MTF Phi-11 ("Promise-Keepers") is to investigate these discussions and make a determination for appropriate action on a case-by-case basis.

Changing this:

MTF Iota-10 ("Damn Feds") are to continue the ongoing investigation regarding the location featured in Addendum 3117.1.

To this:

MTF Iota-10 ("Damn Feds") are to continue the ongoing investigation regarding the location featured in Addendum 3117.1.

Changing this:

In 2006, a member of the Global Occult Coalition's PSYCHE Division contacted Site-95 with the goal of transferring responsibility for containment of "UTE-2639-Pygmalion Blit" over to Foundation care. After several months of negotiation, 378 items (including several hundred documents, digital files, photographs, and one Betamax tape) were taken into Foundation custody. After an extensive clearance process by the on-duty HMCL supervisor, these items have been catalogued as part of SCP-3117's documentation.

To this:

In 2006, a member of the Global Occult Coalition's PSYCHE Division contacted Site-95 with the goal of transferring responsibility for containment of "UTE-2639-Pygmalion Blit" over to Foundation care. After several months of negotiation, 378 items (including several hundred documents, digital files, photographs, and one Betamax tape) were taken into Foundation custody. After an extensive clearance process by the on-duty HMCL supervisor, these items have been catalogued as part of SCP-3117's documentation.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

This is a great concept, and extremely well executed. This is what SCP-2006 should have been. (I'm one of the few who downvoted -that- one.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You made a meta SCP that sort of addresses the user, without the code that displays the username of the reader. Props to ya. I can't think of anything else that hasn't been said, upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Got it. Sorry I must be blind lol, by the way that just adds to the creep factor, I dont often get actual nightmares from stuff, Im pretty desensitized.
You are rapidly becoming one of my favorite authors on the site dude

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No worries! The eyes are just white dots; you might have to look at the full-sized images to see them (IE, open the images by themselves).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry for the semi-necro. This is really creeping the shit out of me, but I feel like I'm missing something, where are the eyes on the spiral staircase pics, I'm staring at them and I can't see them, am I just being incredibly stupid and missing the obvious, or have you changed the pics?
In any case +1, and for the nightmares it's given me…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just like to thank you for both the really cogent explanation and "author headcanon" upthread. The depth and clarity of thought that went into this really emerges in those explanations. I feel the same way about the "comments" on your author page. No criticism intended toward any other authors, but I'd love to see more of this kind of thing. I think it really enhances the experience, for myself anyway. Bravo and +1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oops — yes. Fixing!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the on-duty HCML supervisor

Did you mean the HMCL supervisor?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
TY!

Also — oops — nope, that's definitely a typo. Thank you for fixing it! xD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well you nailed the execution, and the concept. The last interview - I didn't see that twist coming.
One little typo I caught…

//Abruptly, the camera back to the stairs //

I assume 'turned' is supposed to be in there… i hope it's OK if I edited that… If not, apologies.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think you're giving yourself enough credit. :P

Yes, at it's most basic is is 'whatever you think it is'- however, the added concept of something taking advantage of the Foundation's curiosity is what makes it so brilliant in my eyes. As always, I'll look forward to your future work.

(And yes, of course, the execution is what makes it.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is absolutely no bother, and I'm glad it works for you!

It's exceedingly brilliant conceptually.

I don't know about that, I think the idea is basically just a reversed SCP-2006 >_> But this is definitely a skip where the execution is way more important than the concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow wow, now that is scary. Thank you for the explanation, this was really bugging me but now I think I can see it clearly. That's a very clever skip you've got there, and I'm now comfortable with offering a humble upvote. Sorry for the bother.

but I just want to make sure you don't feel obligated to figure out a way to enjoy a story that, honestly, just might not do a darn thing for you. xD

I mean, in a sense, it did do a thing for me- it made me exceedingly frustrated with myself for missing the bigger picture. It's incredibly satisfying now to read over it again with a better understanding, and it really amplifies just how good and immersive your use of images and internal documentation. So don't worry about me forcing myself to like it or something- because I really, really do. It's exceedingly brilliant conceptually.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like this needs to be explained to me like I'm five years old or I'm going to lose my mind, because I love all of your work and I really want to love this, but I just can't grasp what it's getting at.

Okay — first off, I just want to emphasize that it's fine if you don't 'get' it; there might genuinely be nothing for you to 'get'! Horror (and fiction) can be a weird alchemical process — what works for one person just sounds stupid for someone else. I'm happy to take you through what I think is going on if it'll help — but I just want to make sure you don't feel obligated to figure out a way to enjoy a story that, honestly, just might not do a darn thing for you. xD

That being said:

quick rundown
– hide block

it comes across as a bit jarring and unprofessional for a Foundation researcher with many years of hardened experience under their belt to consider these very real events 'scary stories'.

When I said 'effectively read a lot of scary stories', I was using the word 'effectively' to clarify that it doesn't matter if these 'stories' are real or not. Your average Foundation researcher is aware of a lot of scary shit; the fact that they also know it's real just makes it scarier.

How? What? I can't wrap my head around what makes that so 'oh fuck' scary in the first place. So if it's summoned by scary stories you know, so what? What's the danger? Is the strength of the anomaly dependent on how many 'scary stories' the individual knows? Hello?

No — I mean, yes, but not in the way it sounds like you're thinking. SCP-3117 isn't a monster that determines how many scary stories you know and grows in power based on it. It's a space that we project our fears into, and, by doing so, we give it form.

Think of it this way: You're walking toward this pit. What's the scariest thing you can imagine in there? Say it's a pile of flesh-eating bugs. Okay, then — flesh-eating bugs are now in the pit.

Now imagine you're a Foundation researcher. What's the scariest thing you can imagine in there? Probably something way scarier.

The implication here is that the problem is — the reason Jennifer destroyed as many of their notes as they could — is because they realized this thing is, consciously or not, baiting them. The more you research this thing, the more clues you'll find — and these clues are almost custom designed to prompt your imagination into dark places — to make you speculate on what sort of possible horror it is.

It's a spiraling staircase into darkness with a sense of inevitable dread. It's a video-tape with terrifying imagery. It's a sense of being watched, being pursued, being followed — it's an extremely disfigured thing found hiding in a closet.

It's not a monster: It's a series of writing prompts. And your imagination is the author. And whatever horrible thing you're imagining? Whatever story you string together with all of these clues? Whatever monster you use these prompts to create? That's what's coming to get you.

Also, ty for the compliments regarding the pictures! I did put a lot of effort into them. xD Like I said in the author post, this was kind of an experiment on my part — I wanted to see if this sort of thing could really work.

A story that says "The monster is the scariest thing you can imagine" is a really hard sell, imo — so I wanted to see if I could make it work by front-loading it with lots of atmosphere that jump-starts the reader's imagination — until they're in a state when being told "The monster is the scariest thing you can imagine" is genuinely scary, because their imagination is running full-throttle on fear-juice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The images are absolutely spectacular.

That said, the skip itself didn't wow me by any means. It opens an awful lot of loose ends that, at least for me, didn't have a satisfying conclusion beyond 'it's not what you think it is'- which would be excellent if it wasn't already a fairly well-trodden ground. You explore some interesting concepts (I particularly liked the 'scary stories' bit) but at the end of the day I'm left trying to piece together something that I don't think is meant to have a solid conclusion, and that's awfully frustrating.

I also fail to understand the significance of the first image, though that's likely just me being dense.

I don't know. I don't really like to novote anymore, but I'm forced to on the following bases- the effort is staggering and it would feel criminal if I were to downvote something so visually appealing, but I just don't get it. Maybe I'll look back with a fresh pair of eyes and try to make sense of it, but at the moment my head just gels it into a series of events that don't result in anything substantial.

+ show
- hide

Okay so as I was writing this I looked at the previous page of comments and saw your conversation with MacLeod. Consider me even more confused.

You are a Foundation researcher. You have read a lot of articles like this, and therefore have effectively read a lot of scary stories.

That's… a very strange interpretation of the Foundation as a whole. I say interpretation because obviously headcanon exists and blah blah, but I'm left a bit frazzled- it comes across as a bit jarring and unprofessional for a Foundation researcher with many years of hardened experience under their belt to consider these very real events 'scary stories'. Because they're not scary stories. In-universe, they are happening. When I watch a documentary about Jeffrey Dahmer, I don't think 'wow, that was a scary tale', I think, 'wow, some people are fucked'. Blurring the line between real and fake is a dangerous game when we're supposed to immerse ourselves in the idea that all of these documents are about real creatures, objects, and phenomena. Unless what defines a 'scary story' is decided by the anomaly itself and not the individual, in which case… where is the line drawn? Is any spooky event then classed as a 'scary story'?

Now you read this one. This one, which is all about a scary story summoned by scary stories you know.

You are fucking boned.

How? What? I can't wrap my head around what makes that so 'oh fuck' scary in the first place. So if it's summoned by scary stories you know, so what? What's the danger? Is the strength of the anomaly dependent on how many 'scary stories' the individual knows? Hello?

I feel like this needs to be explained to me like I'm five years old or I'm going to lose my mind, because I love all of your work and I really want to love this, but I just can't grasp what it's getting at.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you! And yes, those were meant to be eyes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The date on the photograph is the same as the date on the GOC's report for when the video-tape was found.

Make of that what you will.

item.title: Re: Spoilers in collapsible
item.content:encoded: Ahhh. The horror works on multiple levels, because the way you described the Foundation personnel also applies to members of this site. Gaaaaah.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really fucking interesting and creepy.

Also, when I click on the spiral stairwell images, I notice two white dots. Are those meant to be eyes, because that's really creepy. The context of the image is also really interesting and makes me ask questions. (which is probably a bad thing to do considering the advice at the end of the interview)

1+ because this is really great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mama always told me not to look into the eyes of the sun.
But mama, that's where the fun is.

I can dig it.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As someone who has had multiple nightmares about falling into holes, this one is freaking terrifying. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Context unknown. Found hiding in closet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don’t get the significance of the first image. Any help?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: For your own sake, do not look into the pit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fuck, you caught me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very well done! The last line really works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: always solid editing on those images Hippo. +1

item.title: Re: Spoilers in collapsible
item.content:encoded:
Kind of. Maybe? I don't want to give too much of what I think it is away, but uh —

author headcanon
– hide block

The idea behind Hyakumonogatari Kaidankai is that you tell all these scary stories, and with each tale, you create the right context for an even scarier story to appear — one that's real. Each story is, in a sense, one part of a multi-part evocation to summon a terrifying spirit. Basically: Hear enough scary stories and one might actually emerge.

3117 is something similar: A trap for people who consume scary stories. And both the Foundation and the GOC are full of scary stories — which means this story, whatever it is, would be scary as heck.

ETA: To clarify:

author headcanon+
– hide block

You are a Foundation researcher. You have read a lot of articles like this, and therefore have effectively read a lot of scary stories.

Now you read this one. This one, which is all about a scary story summoned by scary stories you know.

You are fucking boned.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Damn, this is one of my favorites of all the things you’ve written. Fantastic use of images as storytelling components too.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually expected that twist to happen, but there was a solid sense of unease and dread I got from reading this that I'd forgotten it by the end. Good choice to have very little context for what was going on.

+1

item.title: Spoilers in collapsible
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

So it's a monster that only manifests when you know about it? Kind of an antimeme?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well, I picked 7 pictures for a reason xD

There's a lot of additional connections I could have made — I might go back and slip some more in later.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oops; thank you — fixed!

(also: Yes this is specifically meant as a trap for people who read a lot of horror >_>)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is really creepy, and did I catch a subtle connection to SCP-2747?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There’s good pacing, and then there’s me thinking “this is feeling a little too sparse and incomplete” at *exactly* the point I hit “a monster-shaped hole”!

Consider me thoroughly caught by that trap. Nice work!

ETA: in the last interview, Reiner should be Jenner, or vice-versa

item.title: Author's Post
item.content:encoded:
Sweet dreams. ~

SOURCES:

  • Images from here, here, here, here, here, here, here, and here.
  • The 'title card' for the "video" was created by me in GIMP, and something I release wholly into public domain.
  • For a lot of the video effects (static, blotchy colors, etc) I used clips from this video — 'VHS Glitch - Free' — which is offered for free commercial/public use by the author.
  • Inspired by Hyakumonogatari Kaidankai, or A Gathering of One Hundred Supernatural Tales.

Like the article? Click here for more of The Great Hippo's Skippos!
